title: "xorg.conf and nvidia"
date: 2009-06-07
forum: Hardware

### Post by pluMmet on 2009-06-07
when I do a sudo nvidia-conf (not sure it's exactly that) it maks a half assed xorg.conf file

```
# nvidia-xconfig: X configuration file generated by nvidia-xconfig
# nvidia-xconfig:  version 1.0  (buildmeister@builder62)  Wed May 27 01:58:49 PDT 2009

Section "ServerLayout"
    Identifier     "Layout0"
    Screen      0  "Screen0"
    InputDevice    "Keyboard0" "CoreKeyboard"
    InputDevice    "Mouse0" "CorePointer"
EndSection

Section "Files"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Mouse0"
    Driver         "mouse"
    Option         "Protocol" "auto"
    Option         "Device" "/dev/psaux"
    Option         "Emulate3Buttons" "no"
    Option         "ZAxisMapping" "4 5"
EndSection

Section "InputDevice"
    # generated from default
    Identifier     "Keyboard0"
    Driver         "kbd"
EndSection

Section "Monitor"
    Identifier     "Monitor0"
    VendorName     "Unknown"
    ModelName      "Unknown"
    HorizSync       28.0 - 33.0
    VertRefresh     43.0 - 72.0
    Option         "DPMS"
EndSection

Section "Device"
    Identifier     "Device0"
    Driver         "nvidia"
    VendorName     "NVIDIA Corporation"
EndSection

Section "Screen"
    Identifier     "Screen0"
    Device         "Device0"
    Monitor        "Monitor0"
    DefaultDepth    24
    SubSection     "Display"
        Depth       24
    EndSubSection
EndSection

```

I get errors on boot up and have to reset the display. I've installed different nvidia drivers and tried lots of things.

this seems to be the problem?

If some one asked me the boot error Holy Mother it's a ton of stuff that pops up before Ubuntu makes me reset my display driver. Some image missing thing and some tty2 sort of thing.

Any help appreciated.

---

### Post by jbruced on 2009-06-07
In a nutshell what worked for me.

Make sure you have nvidia-settings, if not get it.
At a terminal:

sudo nvidia-settings

poke around the gui to set up what you need,
click apply
click save settings
click quit

now restart Ubuntu before doing anything else.

Hope it works for you

Bruce

---

### Post by pluMmet on 2009-06-07
I can't use nvidia-settings. I get this error:
```
You do not appear to be using the NVIDIA X driver. Please edit your X configuration file (just run `nvidia-xconfig` as root), and restart the X server. 
```

and when I run `nvidia-xconfig it makes a not so usfull xorg.conf file that I listed.

### Post by CatKiller on 2009-06-08
> **pluMmet said:**
> I can't use nvidia-settings. I get this error:
```
You do not appear to be using the NVIDIA X driver. Please edit your X configuration file (just run `nvidia-xconfig` as root), and restart the X server. 
```and when I run `nvidia-xconfig it makes a not so usfull xorg.conf file that I listed.

You can always make the changes yourself without using NVidia's tool.

IIRC, the changes are to put ```
        Driver          "nvidia"
```in the "Device" Section, commenting out any commands to load the "GLcore" and "dri" modules and adding in ```
        Load    "glx"
``` to the "Module" Section.

I noticed that you've got HorizSync and VertRefresh ranges specified. If those numbers are accurate and you find that you can't get the correct resolution, you might need to put those in too.
